title: Neoliberalism, the Alt-right and the Intellectual Dark Web
author: Finlayson

opening_move.description: Existing data-driven studies of the online political right map communities and platforms but fail to analyse the underlying ideological architecture. Finlayson proposes to complement these with an ideological-historical analysis centred on the concept of 'ideological entrepreneurs' and the shared anti-egalitarian metaphysics uniting Alt-right, Alt-lite, and Intellectual Dark Web.
opening_move.stakes_claimed: Understanding how disparate online reactionary tendencies (paleoconservatism, race-realism, sex-realism, libertarianism, neoreaction) cohere around a common hostility to egalitarianism is essential for diagnosing the ideological character of post-neoliberal right politics.

rivals_engaged: simple descriptions of the Alt-right as white nationalism
theoretical_positioning.description: The paper argues against reducing the online right to community or platform data, and proposes that ideological analysis reveals the deep common structure: a shared claim that natural inequalities (of race, sex) are real and that liberal egalitarianism is a denial of nature enforced by a 'new class' of cultural managers. The 'red pill' trope, the 'Cultural Marxist' conspiracy, and 'ideological entrepreneurship' are the key mechanisms binding these tendencies together.

epistemic_mode.qualitative_methods: Textual and discursive analysis of websites, YouTube channels, manifestos, and publications of alt-right, alt-lite, IDW, and paleoconservative actors (American Renaissance, VDare, Radix Journal, Stefan Molyneux, Jordan Peterson, Paul Joseph Watson, etc.); intellectual-historical genealogy tracing concepts (new class, cultural Marxism, race-realism) from paleoconservatism and neoconservatism to contemporary online spaces.
epistemic_mode.interpretive_methods: Ideological analysis and intellectual genealogy: tracing the transmission of anti-egalitarian political ideas (Burnham, Kristol, Gottfried, de Benoist) into online reactionary spaces; rhetorical analysis of 'red pill' narrative and 'Cultural Marxist' trope.
epistemic_mode.notes: The paper is a normative-interpretive ideological analysis with constitutive textual evidence: specific websites, publications, and figures are the primary material. The argument is that the online right has an identifiable ideological grammar (anti-egalitarianism, new class critique, nature-as-truth) that transcends platform differences.

case_type.case_name: Alt-right, Alt-lite, and Intellectual Dark Web online ecosystem

normative_stake.domain: political sociology, ideology critique, right-wing politics

confidence_notes: This paper is a normative-interpretive ideological analysis with constitutive textual evidence from online reactionary sources. The epistemic mode is normative_with_interpretive_case where the case (the alt-right ecosystem) is both the object of analysis and provides the evidential material. The move sequence reflects: gap in community-mapping approaches → propose ideological analysis → genealogical reconstruction of anti-egalitarian ideas → case development through specific actors → interpretation of common grammar (red pill, Cultural Marxism) → open conclusion.
